Translation of the "Little Prince" into Mokums, a dialect spoken mainly in the former working-class neighborhoods of
Amsterdam. The dialect was mainly passed on orally and there are virtually no written records of language rules, as is the case with ABN (Algemeen Beschaafd Nederlands), for example.

This literary project was led by the French Honorary Consul in Wales, Céline Jones, and the Welsh poet Archippus Sturrock, known by his pen name RJ Arkhipov.

littlePrinceItem.title:
Lou Pitióu Princi
Der kleine Prinz - Cuquois/Tarnais
littlePrinceItem.description: Translation of the "Little Prince" into Occitan. Occitan is a Romance language traditionally spoken in the southern third of France, as well as in several Italian valleys and in the Val d'Aran (Spain). It is commonly distinguished into six major Occitan dialects: Auvergnatic, Gascon, Languedocian, Limousin, Provençal, and Vivaro-Alpine. The Cuquois or parler de Cuq (Cuq Les Vielmur, Tarn) into which this version of the Little Prince has been translated is a variety of Languedocien (Albigensian subdialect). More precisely, the following translation was produced in the dialect of the Alrié farm (l'Alriè).

littlePrinceItem.title:
L' P'tit Prince
Der kleine Prinz - Beaunois
littlePrinceItem.description:
Übersetzung des "Kleinen Prinzen" in einen Dialekt des Croissant.

The Croissant languages are traditionally spoken in central France, on the northern fringe of the Massif Central. Part of the Gallo-Romance language family, they simultaneously display many features of Occitan (Limousin or Auvergnat) and Oïl languages (French, Berrichon, Bourbonnais or Poitevin-Saintongeais). Beaunois or Beaune-d'Allier (Allier) speech, into which this version of The Little Prince is translated, is a variety of the eastern part of the Crescent (linguistic area of Bourbonnais d'oc). More specifically, the following translation was produced in the vernacular of the hamlet of Ladoux (Ladou).

littlePrinceItem.title: Der kleine Prinz in deutschen Mundarten - Jubiläumsausgabe
littlePrinceItem.description:
With the present anniversary edition of "The Little Prince in German Dialects"
Edition Tintenfaß is presenting the 200th Little Prince edition in foreign languages
of the world and in numerous (not only German) dialects.
The total of 29 chapters of the "Little Prince" (including the dedication
and an epilogue) are each dedicated to a different variety of German, beginning with the High German version of the dedication ("Für
Léon Werth") and then, progressively per chapter, selected Low German,
Middle German and Upper German dialects. Also taken into account are
German dialects spoken outside of Germany, in France (Alsace), Switzerland
France (Alsace), Switzerland, Belgium, Austria, Italy (South Tyrol), Brazil and
the United States.

Der kleine Prinz - Wallisianisch (Faka’uvea)
littlePrinceItem.description: Translation of the Little Prince into Faka'uvea (Wallisian), a Polynesian language spoken on the island of Wallis in the French overseas "collectivité" of Wallis and Futuna.

littlePrinceItem.title:
Le P'tit Prince
Der kleine Prinz - Croissant (Saint-Léginaud)
littlePrinceItem.translator: Dauby, Monique; Dauby, Marie-José; Dauby, Jean-Michel
littlePrinceItem.description:
Übersetzung des "Kleinen Prinzen" in einen Dialekt des Croissant.

The Croissant languages are traditionally spoken in central France, on the northern fringe of the Massif Central. Part of the Gallo-Romance language family, they simultaneously display many of the characteristics of Occitan (Limousin or Auvergnat) and Oïl languages (French, Berrichon, Bourbonnais or Poitevin-Saintongeais). Saint-Léginaud, or the language of Saint-Léger-Magnazeix (Haute-Vienne), into which this version of The Little Prince is translated, is a variety from the western part of the Crescent (Marchois linguistic area). More precisely, Saint-Léginaud is a representative variety of Bas-Marchois, a variant of Marchois spoken in northern Haute-Vienne (on the right bank of the Gartempe) and in several neighboring communes in the Creuse (La Souterraine region).

littlePrinceItem.title:
Wawa Príncipe
Der kleine Prinz - Afro-bolivianisches Spanisch (Afroyungueno)
littlePrinceItem.translator: Vásquez, Ingrid Marín; Iriondo, Viviana Barra; Sessarego, Sandro
littlePrinceItem.description: Translation of The Little Prince into Bolivian Afro-Spanish, spoken by the descendants of African slaves in Bolivia. All illustrations have been redesigned by a Bolivian artist and adapted to the Afro-Bolivian context. A wonderful new pictorial and linguistic perspective on the Little Prince.

littlePrinceItem.title: 小王子 (Xiǎo wángzǐ) / Le Petit Prince
littlePrinceItem.subTitle: zweisprachig: Chinesisch und Französisch
littlePrinceItem.description: New translation of the "Little Prince" into Chinese by the well-known Germanist and award-winning translator Wei Maoping, Professor Emeritus of the "Shanghai International Studies University" in Shanghai, winner of the "Goldene Goethe-Medaille" of the Goethe Society Weimar.

littlePrinceItem.title: 星の王子さま (Hoshinoōjisama) / Le Petit Prince
littlePrinceItem.subTitle: zweisprachig: Japanisch und Französisch
littlePrinceItem.description: Translation of the "Little Prince" into Japanese by the well-known Japanese poet Durian Sukegawa. Bilingual edition with the French original.

littlePrinceItem.title:
Le Ch'ti Prince
Der kleine Prinz - Croissant (Barberier)
littlePrinceItem.description:
Translation of "The Little Prince" into a dialect of the French Croissant region.

Crescent languages are traditionally spoken in central France, on the northern fringe of the Massif Central.
As part of the Gallo-Romance language family, they simultaneously present
Occitan (Limousin or Auvergnat), as well as Oïl language (Limousin or Auvergnat) characteristics (French, Berrichon, Bourbonnais or Poitevin-Saintongeais). The Barberier language
(Allier), into which this version of "The Little Prince" is translated, is a variety from the eastern part of the Croissant (Bourbonnais d'oc linguistic area).

littlePrinceItem.title:
Lë Pitit Prince
Der kleine Prinz - Lesterrois
littlePrinceItem.translator: Desnoël-Savy, Marguerite; Quériaud, Jean-Louis; Quint, Nicolas
littlePrinceItem.description: Occitan is a Romance language that is traditionally spoken in the southern third of France as well as in several Italian valleys and in the Val d'Aran (Spain). There are generally six major Occitan dialects: Auvergne, Gascon, Languedocien, Limousin, Provençal and Vivaro-Alpine. Lesterrois or parler de Lesterps (Charente), into which this version of The Little Prince has been translated, is a variety of Limousin (sub-dialect of Haut-Limousin). More precisely, this translation comes from the hamlet of La Motte (La Mouto).

littlePrinceItem.title:
언나 왕자 (Eonna Wangja)
Der kleine Prinz - Koreanisch (Gangwon-do)
littlePrinceItem.description: Translation of "The Little Prince" into Gangwon-do, a dialect spoken in the province of Gangwon-do in the north-east of South Korea and in the North Korean province of Kangwŏn.

littlePrinceItem.title:
De kleine Prins / Le Petit Prince
zweisprachig: Niederländisch und Französisch
littlePrinceItem.description: New translation of the "Little Prince" into Dutch. Bilingual edition: Dutch and French.

littlePrinceItem.title: ءَالْ بّْرِنْسِّبِّتٝ (El Principito)
littlePrinceItem.description: "The Little Prince", translated into Español aljamiado, a variant of Spanish used in the late Middle Ages on the Iberian Peninsula by the "Morisco" and "Mudéjar" populations and written in Arabic script. The text is accompanied by a version in modern standard Spanish, which can be found on the same page.

littlePrinceItem.title: КИШКЕЙ ХАН БАЛАСЫ (Kişkey han balası)
littlePrinceItem.description: "The Little Prince" translates into Nogaic, a Turkic language spoken by around 80,000 people in the north-eastern Caucasus.

littlePrinceItem.title:
Le Petit Prince en Langues de France
Métropole et outre-mer
littlePrinceItem.description:
In this multilingual edition, The Little Prince speaks 32 languages of France, one per chapter (and in the dedication and epilogue).
We are especially proud that we were able to publish this book in cooperation with the "Cité internationale de la langue française" in Villers-Cotterêts, France and the "Centre des monuments nationaux". Accompagnied by a foreword by Paul de Sinety, the "Délégué général à la langue française et aux langues de France" of the French "Ministère de la Culture" in Paris.

littlePrinceItem.title: Le P'tit Prince
littlePrinceItem.subTitle: Der kleine Prinz - Croissant (Champagne et Chassî)
littlePrinceItem.translator: Pouvaraud, Marie-France; Vidaud-Richard, Anne-Marie; Quint, Nicolas
littlePrinceItem.description: The Croissant languages are traditionally spoken on the northern edge of the Massif Central. As part of the Gallo-Romance language family, they have many features of Occitan (Limousin or Auvergnat), as well as Oïl languages (French, Berrichon, Bourbonnais or Poitevin-Saintongeais). The dialect of Champagne-Mouton and Chassiecq (Charente), into which this version of The Little Prince has been translated, is a variety of the western part of Croissant (Marchois language area). More precisely, the following translation was made in the language of the hamlets of Chez Pouvaraud (Châ Pouvaréé) for the commune of Champagne-Mouton and La Chaume (La Chaume) for the commune of Chassiecq.

littlePrinceItem.title: Lé Pétit Prïnce
littlePrinceItem.subTitle: Der kleine Prinz - Patois des Vosges Méridionales
littlePrinceItem.description: Translation of "The Little Prince" into the dialect (langue d'oïl) of the Val d'Ajol in the southern Vosges.

littlePrinceItem.title: De klääne Prinz
littlePrinceItem.subTitle: Der kleine Prinz - Lothringer Platt
littlePrinceItem.description: Translation of "The Little Prince" into the Rhine-Franconian dialect of Lorraine (Département Moselle/ Grand Est)

littlePrinceItem.title:
Le P'ti Prince
Der kleine Prinz - Parler de Saint Brabant et Saint-Martial-sur-Isop (Croissant)
littlePrinceItem.translator: Chassat, Marie-Jo; Chassat, Moïse; Chassat, Pierre; Chegaray, Henriette; Maisonnier, Annie; Ranger, Olivier; Maisonnier, Armand; Allassonnière-Tang, Marc; Quint, Nicolas
littlePrinceItem.description: The "Croissant" languages are traditionally spoken in central France, on the edge of the northern side of the Massif Central. As part of the Gallo-Romance language family, they have characteristics of Occitan (Limousin or Auvergnat), but also of the langues d'oïl (French, Berrichon, Bourbonnais or Poitevin-Saintongeais). This variety is spoken in small villages in the department of Haute-Vienne in the Nouvelle-Aquitaine region of south-western France.

littlePrinceItem.title:
어린왕자 (Eorin Wangja)
Der kleine Prinz - Chungcheongnam-do (Koreanisch)
littlePrinceItem.description: Translation of the classic into the dialect of the Korean province of Chungcheongnam-do. The project was initiated and sponsored by the "ChungCheongNam-Do Economic Promotion Agency, Chungnam Frankfurt Office" in Frankfurt am Main. With a foreword by the Governor of Chungcheongnam-do Province, Mr. Tae Heum Kim.

littlePrinceItem.title:
T’ Little Prince
Der kleine Prinz - Yorkshire Dialect
littlePrinceItem.description: Translation of the classic into the dialect of Yorkshire, Bradford, West Riding, a variety of English spoken in the western part of County Yorkshire in the north of England.

littlePrinceItem.title: El Princhipiko - Der kleine Prinz - Ladino
littlePrinceItem.subTitle: Trezladado del franzes al ladino por Avner Perez i Gladys Pimienta (in lateinischer und Rashi-Schrift)
littlePrinceItem.description: “The Little Prince“ translated into Ladino (Judeo-Spanish), the endangered language of Sephardic Jews.

littlePrinceItem.title: Le Petit Prince - Binaire
littlePrinceItem.description: “The Little Prince” - for the very first time in binary code! The individual characters of the original French text have been broken down into bits and bytes using the American Standard Code for Information Interchange (ASCII) and the ISO-8859-1 (Latin-1, Western European) coding standard and are represented in 0s or 1s.

littlePrinceItem.title: Le Petit Prince - Polyglotte (29 languages)
littlePrinceItem.description: Antoine de Saint-Exupéry's "The Little Prince" is a truly international book and, next to the Bible, the world’s most translated work of literature. This polyglot edition celebrates this beloved classic’s internationality by assigning one language to each of its 29 chapters (including the dedication and epilogue), presenting an impressive global linguistic kaleidoscope, ranging from Anishinaabemowin to Yidish, from Ancient Egyptian to Wolof and from Classical Arabic to Ukrainian, including some indigenous as well as historical languages.

littlePrinceItem.title:
De kleine preens
Der kleine Prinz - Stellingwarfs
littlePrinceItem.description: Translation of the classic into Stellingwarfs, a variety of Dutch Low Saxon (Nedersaksisch), which is spoken in the south of the province of Friesland (Fryslân) in the Netherlands.

littlePrinceItem.title:
Dea kloine Prinz
Der kleine Prinz - Egerländer Mundart
littlePrinceItem.description: Translation of the classic into the Egerland dialect, a Bavarian dialect that was spoken between Eger, Karlsbad and Pilsen before the expulsion of ethnic Germans from the “Sudetenland” after 1945. The area of the historical Egerland is now mostly in the Czech Republic.

littlePrinceItem.title:
Fende’ fe Wye Ntu’
Der kleine Prinz - Ga'a Kejom (Babanki, Kamerun)
littlePrinceItem.description: Translation of the classic into Babanki, a bantoid language spoken by about 20,000 people of the Babanki ethnic group from the Cameroon grasslands.

littlePrinceItem.title:
U príncipi piquinínu
Der kleine Prinz - Purtuguês-Oliventinu
littlePrinceItem.description: Translation of the classic into the Portuguese dialect Oliventinu, which is spoken in south-west Spain on the border with Portugal around the town of Olivenza.

littlePrinceItem.title:
Le Prinçòt
Der kleine Prinz - Gascon
littlePrinceItem.description: Translation of the classic into Gascon, a variety of Occitan spoken in the South of France, west of Toulouse.

littlePrinceItem.title:
Wë Petit Prince
Der kleine Prinz - Paicî (Kanak)
littlePrinceItem.description: Translation of the classic into one of the 28 (!) languages of the Kanak, the indigenous Melanesian people of New Caledonia in the south-west Pacific. The language group belongs to the oceanic languages and has around 7,000 speakers, most of whom live in the northern province of the island.

littlePrinceItem.title:
Nokon Than
Der kleine Prinz - Iaai (Kanak)
littlePrinceItem.description:
Translation of the classic into one of the 28 (!) languages of the Kanak, the indigenous Melanesian natives of New Caledonia in the south-west Pacific. The 
language group belongs to the oceanic languages and has around 4,000 speakers who live on the island of Ouvéa.

littlePrinceItem.title:
Lu P'tit Prince
Der kleine Prinz - Champenois
littlePrinceItem.description: Raise your champagne glasses and welcome  this translation of the classic into the regional language of Champagne in north-east France and a small region in southern Belgium. À votre santé!

littlePrinceItem.title:
Prinsizinh
Der kleine Prinz - Kreol von São Nicolau (Kapverden)
littlePrinceItem.translator: da Luz Morais, Rosa; Quint, Nicolas
littlePrinceItem.description: Translation of the classic into a variant of the Portuguese-based Creole language of Cape Verde, which is spoken on the island of São Nicolau.

littlePrinceItem.title:
Le P'tit Prince
Der kleine Prinz - Poitevin de Genouillé
littlePrinceItem.description: Translation of the classic into a langue d'oïl spoken in the west of central France.

littlePrinceItem.title: Le Petit Prince dans les parlers du Croissant
littlePrinceItem.subTitle: Aux confins de l'occitan et des langues d'oïl
littlePrinceItem.description:
This book takes us on a special journey through the linguistic landscape of the Croissant. The languages of the Croissant are traditionally spoken on the northern edge of the Massif Central. As part of the Gallo-Romance language family, they also have numerous characteristics of Occitan (Limousin or Auvergnat), but also of the Oïl languages  (French, Berrichon, Bourbonnais or Poitevin-Saintongeais). 
Poitevin-Saintongeais). Each chapter of Antoine de Saint-Exupéry's “The Little Prince” is found in its own variety and thus gives an insight into the extraordinary diversity that characterizes the croissant.

littlePrinceItem.title:
Da kleene Prinz
Der kleine Prinz - Sudetenschlesisch
littlePrinceItem.description: This edition is an anthology of “The Little Prince” in 18 different Silesian-German dialects spoken in the former “Sudetenland”, which was mainly inhabited by ethnic Germans in what is now the Czech Republic and Slovakia.

littlePrinceItem.title:
Dr klejne Prinz
Der kleine Prinz - Paurisch (Gablonzer Mundart)
littlePrinceItem.description: Paurisch is the name of the German dialect formerly spoken by the inhabitants of Gablonz (today: Jablonec nad Nisou, Czech Republic). Today it has survived in Neu-Gablonz in Bavaria, where many former inhabitants of Gablonz found a new home after being expelled after the Second World War.

littlePrinceItem.title:
De klaane Prinz
Der kleine Prinz - Schwowisch (Banatschwäbisch)
littlePrinceItem.description: This German dialect was (and to a certain extent still is) spoken in the Banat region, a German-speaking enclave in Romania.

littlePrinceItem.title:
Le P'tit Prince
Der kleine Prinz - Croissant (Mouhetin)
littlePrinceItem.description: The languages of the “Croissant” are traditionally spoken in central France, on the northern edge of the Massif Central. As part of the Gallo-Romance language family, they exhibit characteristics of both Occitan (Limousin or Auvergnat) and the langues d'oïl (French, Berrichon, Bourbonnais, or Poitevin-Saintongeais). This variety is spoken in Mouhet, a village in the Indre department in central France.

littlePrinceItem.title:
Кънѧжѧ (Knęžę)
Der kleine Prinz - Altkirchenslawisch
littlePrinceItem.description: A translation of the classic into Old Church Slavonic, the oldest Slavic literary language, which dates back to Saints Cyril and Methodius in the 9th century and is still used as a liturgical language in some Orthodox churches. It is written in the Cyrillic alphabet.

littlePrinceItem.title:
Der kleine Prinz / Le Petit Prince
Der kleine Prinz - zweisprachig: Deutsch & Französisch
littlePrinceItem.description: Exclusive special edition on the occasion of the opening of the museum “The Little Prince and His World” in Solothurn, Switzerland. Not available in bookstores. The book will be available for purchase exclusively at the museum, starting in November 2025.

littlePrinceItem.title:
Lou Pechot Prince
Der kleine Prinz - Provençal alpin
littlePrinceItem.description: Translation of the classic into the Occitan dialect spoken in the mountainous regions around the town of Gap in the Hautes-Alpes department in southeastern France. It is considered a “langue menacée de disparition” (language threatened with extinction).

littlePrinceItem.title:
족은 왕자 (Jogen Wangja)
Der kleine Prinz - Jejueo
littlePrinceItem.description: Translation of the classic into Jejueo, a Korean language spoken on Jeju Island off the south coast of South Korea. In 2010, there were still 5,000 speakers, but even then it was classified by UNESCO as “endangered,” which is the highest level of language endangerment.

littlePrinceItem.title:
ВикIазив талхъан (Wik'aziw talqan)
Der kleine Prinz - Kubachi
littlePrinceItem.description: Translation of the classic into a Northeast Caucasian language spoken in a village in the southern part of the Republic of Dagestan in the Russian Federation by only 7,000 people. The number of speakers is currently declining dramatically.

littlePrinceItem.title:
Le Petit Prince
Der kleine Prinz - Poitevin de Clairin et la Barre de Sepvret
littlePrinceItem.description: Translation of the classic into the Patois de Poitevin de Clairin et La Barre de Sepvret, a variety of the western French language Poitevin, spoken in two small villages southwest of the city of Poitiers.

littlePrinceItem.title:
Le P'tit Prince
Der kleine Prinz - Parler d’Availles-Limouzine (Croissant)
littlePrinceItem.description: Translation of the classic into a language of the ‘Croissant’. These are traditionally spoken in central France, on the northern edge of the Massif Central. As part of the Gallo-Roman language family, they exhibit characteristics of both Occitan (Limousin or Auvergnat) and the langues d'oïl (French, Berrichon, Bourbonnais or Poitevin-Saintongeais). This dialect is spoken in a small village in the Vienne department.

littlePrinceItem.title:
Lo Ptét Prïnce
Der kleine Prinz - Jurassien
littlePrinceItem.description: Translation of the classic into a variety spoken by fewer than 3,000 people in the Swiss canton of Jura. Jurassien was recently recognised as a minority language in Switzerland.

littlePrinceItem.title:
الْأَمِيرْ الصْغِيوَرْ (El Amir Es Sghiwar)
Der kleine Prinz - Parler Tlemcénien - Algérie
littlePrinceItem.description: Translation of the classic into an Arabic dialect spoken in the Algerian city of Tlemcen.
